content: 6 min read 1 hour ago -- Press enter or click to view image in full size We are in the age of vibe-coding. AI generates components, scaffolds pages, wires up routes. Features ship faster than ever. But there is a question most teams skip: will this actually perform at production level, on a real device, on a real network? The default answer is lazy loading and code splitting. Both are fine. Neither is enough. The real gains come from reading the browser’s own reporting honestly. Chrome DevTools has been quietly getting better at telling you exactly what is slow and exactly why. This article covers the metrics you need to understand and two concrete techniques that move the numbers. First, the metrics — what the browser is actually measuring There are six numbers worth knowing. Three of them directly affect your Google search ranking. The other three help you diagnose why those three are bad. FP — First Paint. The first pixel drawn — even a background colour. Tells you the blank white screen is over. Good: < 1s FCP — First Contentful Paint. First real text or image visible. In a client-side React app, this is late because the browser must download and run your entire JS bundle before React renders anything. Good: ≤ 1.8s LCP — Largest Contentful Paint. When the biggest element in the viewport — usually your hero image or main heading — finishes painting. Google’s primary load speed signal. Good: ≤ 2.5s DCL — DOMContentLoaded. The HTML is parsed and the DOM is built. In a React SPA, this is delayed until React’s synchronous execution finishes. A DCL above 5 seconds means your UI was blocked for that entire duration. Good: < 2s CLS — Cumulative Layout Shift. A score measuring how much content unexpectedly jumps around. Images without dimensions, banners injecting above existing content, fonts swapping — all score against you. Good: ≤ 0.1 INP — Interaction to Next Paint. How long from a user click or keypress to the next visual update. Replaced FID in 2024 because it measures every interaction, not just the first. A single heavy React re-render cascade will destroy your INP. Good: ≤ 200ms LCP, INP, and CLS are Core Web Vitals — the three metrics Google uses for search ranking. FP, FCP, and DCL are diagnostic tools that explain why those three are suffering. How to read them in Chrome DevTools Open the Performance tab, tick Disable cache in the Network tab first, set CPU to 4× slowdown in Performance settings, then hit Start profiling and reload page. Once recorded, look at the Timings row. The LCP marker shows you exactly when it fired and — critically — which element the browser considered the largest. Click it. DevTools highlights the element. If that element appears late in the network waterfall, sitting behind 20 other requests, you have found your problem. Localhost numbers are fiction. Always profile against npm run preview — your actual production build — not npm run dev. The dev bundle is unminified, enormous, and gives you falsely slow measurements that do not reflect what users receive. Technique 1 — preload the LCP element The browser discovers resources as it parses HTML. If your hero image lives inside a React component, the browser does not know it exists until JavaScript downloads, executes, and React renders. By that point you are already 3–4 seconds in. The LCP element was discovered far too late. Press enter or click to view image in full size LCP without Pre LoadPress enter or click to view image in full size LCP getting fetched at a later endThe fix is a single line in your index.html head: This tells the browser: fetch this at maximum priority before you even start parsing the JavaScript bundle. The image is in flight from the first millisecond. Then on the image element itself, never use loading=”lazy” on your hero. Lazy loading is for images below the fold. On the LCP element it is actively harmful: The width and height attributes matter for a second reason: they let the browser reserve the correct space before the image loads. Without them, the image appearing causes a layout shift — a CLS penalty on top of your LCP problem. Press enter or click to view image in full size LCP with priority prefetchPress enter or click to view image in full size LCP getting prefetchedIn practice, preloading the LCP image combined with converting it from JPEG to WebP (25–35% smaller at equivalent quality) typically moves a Poor LCP score into Good without touching a single line of React code. Technique 2 — upgrade from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 This is the least talked about and one of the highest-leverage changes you can make. HTTP/1.1 processes one request per TCP connection. Browsers work around this by opening 6–8 parallel connections per origin — but each connection still needs its own TLS handshake, and requests still queue. If your code-split React app produces 30 chunk files, they load in batches. Each batch waits for the previous. Press enter or click to view image in full size LCP with HTTP 1.1HTTP/2 sends all 30 requests over a single connection simultaneously. One TLS handshake. No queue. The difference is measurable on any page with more than 10 resources — which is every modern React app. Press enter or click to view image in full size LCP with HTTP upgrade to HTTP 2/3How to check which version you are on Open DevTools → Network tab → right-click the column headers → enable Protocol. If you see http/1.1 next to your assets, you are leaving performance on the table. If you are on Vercel, Netlify, or Cloudflare — HTTP/2 is already on. If you are self-hosting on a VPS with Nginx, adding http2 to the listen directive is a one-line change with zero application code required. HTTP/3 (QUIC) goes further by eliminating TCP’s head-of-line blocking entirely, which matters most on unreliable mobile networks. Cloudflare enables it automatically for any proxied domain. Upgrading from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 on a page with 30+ assets typically cuts total load time by 20–40%. The gain is largest on code-split React apps with many small chunk files — exactly what Vite produces. The process, not the one-time fix Performance is not a task you complete. It is a measurement loop. The two techniques here — preloading LCP and enabling HTTP/2 — are the highest-leverage load-time improvements available with the least code change. The next article in this series will be on INP: profiling React re-renders with the React DevTools Profiler, finding the commits that take over 50ms, and eliminating unnecessary renders with React.memo and useCallback.
